did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based deep learning model to classify and recognize seven types of skin cancer lesions (including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.) from the HAM10000 dataset. The study achieved 99% training accuracy and 96% validation accuracy using this AI method.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom 12-layer CNN model on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific new results, including 99% training accuracy and 96% validation accuracy, and details the methodology, hyperparameters, and comparative analysis, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Ensemble of CNN based Deep Learning model for the Recognition of Skin Cancer Images
journal: 2024 IEEE 5th India Council International Subsections Conference (INDISCON)
brief_summary: This study proposes a custom 12-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify seven types of skin cancer lesions, including melanoma, using the publicly available HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involves preprocessing and data augmentation techniques to handle class imbalance, followed by training the model with the Adam optimizer and a learning rate of 0.001 for 50 epochs. The dataset was split into an 80:20 ratio for training and validation. The model achieved a training accuracy of 99% and a validation accuracy of 96% at the final epoch. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed CNN architecture for the early detection and recognition of skin cancer images.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin cancer lesions (Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a CNN model to classify seven types of skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The task is explicitly described as 'classification' and 'recognition' of discrete diagnostic categories. The model outputs class predictions with reported training accuracy of 99% and validation accuracy of 96%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset containing 10,015 skin cancer images. The dataset was used for the classification of seven types of skin lesions. Data augmentation was applied to handle class imbalance and evenly distribute data.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model after an 80:20 split for training and validation.
use.validation: Used for validation of the model after an 80:20 split for training and validation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are reported as descriptive accuracy metrics (99% training accuracy, 96% validation accuracy) without statistical inference.

HAM10000.dataset_description: Publicly available benchmark dataset containing 10,015 skin cancer images. Divided into training (80%) and validation (20%) datasets after data augmentation.
HAM10000.experiment: Custom 12-layer CNN with Adam optimizer, learning rate 0.001, 50 epochs

Custom 12-layer CNN.loss_notes: Training loss close to 0 on the last epoch. Validation loss decreased and then increased slightly before final epoch.

HAM10000.notes: The model was trained on Google Colab. The validation accuracy initially decreased (epochs 20-30) before increasing to 96% at epoch 50. The paper states the architecture obtained 'significantly good results' for skin cancer image recognition.

layer_types: Convolutional layers (with filters/kernels for feature extraction)
Pooling layers (for reducing load and overfitting)
Flattening layer (to convert data to one-dimensional vector)
activation_functions: Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) on dense layers

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with softmax (implied by multi-class classification context)
classification_or_output_head.neurons: Number of neurons corresponds to the number of classes (7)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: FCNs based on VGG16 and GoogleNet
Transfer learning with pretrained deep neural network
First Segmentation (ABCD) applied with various algorithms like Watershed, GLCM and feature extraction using ABCD rule and later SVM
Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) via KNIME Analytics Platform Software
preprocessing_techniques: Image sorting into respective folders by class
Data augmentation to handle class imbalance and evenly distribute data

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the HAM10000 dataset to handle class imbalance and evenly distribute data between training and validation sets. Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation, flipping, scaling) were not reported in the text.

conclusions: The proposed custom 12-layer Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model effectively identifies and classifies seven types of skin lesions (Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions, Melanoma, and Melanocytic Nevus) using the HAM10000 dataset.
The model achieved a training accuracy of 99% and a validation accuracy of 96% after 50 epochs, demonstrating its efficiency for skin cancer image recognition.
The study confirms that deep learning-based models can reduce the difficulty of differentiating between similar skin lesion types, thereby facilitating early detection and timely treatment.
Data augmentation was necessary to handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, allowing for even distribution between training and validation sets.
The Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001 was identified as the optimal configuration for the proposed model.
The research supports the use of machine learning techniques to automate the analysis of skin diseases, potentially reducing mortality rates and errors caused by human fatigue.
important_remarks: The study lacks an independent external test set; the dataset was split only into training (80%) and validation (20%) sets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to unseen populations.

Data augmentation was applied to handle class imbalance, but specific augmentation techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, scaling parameters) were not reported, raising concerns about reproducibility and potential data leakage if augmentation statistics were derived from the validation set.

The paper does not report standard classification metrics beyond accuracy, such as precision, recall, F1-score, or confusion matrices, which are critical for evaluating performance on imbalanced classes like melanoma.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported accuracy improvements over baseline or comparative models.

The validation accuracy fluctuated significantly during training (dropping to 65% at epoch 30 before rising to 96%), suggesting potential instability or overfitting risks that are not fully addressed.

The model was trained on a single public dataset (HAM10000) using Google Colab hardware, with no mention of cross-validation or multi-center validation, which restricts clinical relevance and robustness claims.
The paper claims 'significantly good results' but provides no comparative analysis against state-of-the-art models on the same train/test split to substantiate the claim of superiority.
